one_line_takeaway: SPIDER-SENSE makes agent safety intrinsic and selective — the agent self-triggers a hierarchical similarity-then-reasoning check only when it senses risk — cutting attack success and false positives with far lower latency than always-on guardrails, with the strongest gains on the authors' own S2Bench benchmark.
key_finding: On the authors' own S2Bench (Table 3, Total), SPIDER-SENSE attains the lowest ASR and FPR of all methods: Spider-Sense(Qwen-max) ASR 13.6 / FPR 10.4 at +8% latency (23.4s), and Spider-Sense(Claude-3.5) ASR 9.5 / FPR 19.1 at +37% (41.7s) — versus GuardAgent +251%/+197% and AGrail +381%/+298% latency overhead. Ablations show removing action-stage sensing raises ASR by 29.9 points and that both HAS tiers are necessary (fine-grained for precision, coarse-grained for efficiency). On external benchmarks the result is competitive/mixed: Mind2Web(Claude-3.5) LPA 84.8→95.8 and eICU 78.6→96.7 over the pure-model baseline, but AGrail(Claude-3.5) beats SPIDER-SENSE on Mind2Web F1 (94.1 vs 92.1).
core_contribution: SPIDER-SENSE is an event-driven defense framework for LLM-based autonomous agents built on two of the paper's own novel components. (1) Intrinsic Risk Sensing (IRS): rather than the prevailing "mandatory checking" paradigm that forcibly runs a security check at every predefined lifecycle stage, IRS uses instruction-level conditioning to make the agent itself emit a stage-specific risk-sensing indicator (via structured templates such as <|verify_user_intent|>, <|validate_memory_plan|>, <|audit_action_parameters|>, <|sanitize_observation|>) across four security-critical stages (query, plan, action, observation), pausing execution only when risk is actually perceived. (2) Hierarchical Adaptive Screening (HAS): once triggered, the flagged artifact is routed to a stage-specific inspector that first does fast coarse-grained cosine-similarity matching against per-stage attack-pattern vector databases, and escalates to slower fine-grained LLM reasoning (over top-K retrieved cases) only when similarity falls below a threshold; the agent then returns ACCEPT/REJECT/SANITIZE. The paper also contributes S2Bench, a lifecycle-aware benchmark. NOTE: conceptually similar prior methods (ALRPHFS's adversarially-learned risk-pattern library + hierarchical fast/slow reasoning; AGrail's adaptive guardrail) are cited RELATED WORK and used as baselines — not this paper's contribution.
threat_model: Adversarial manipulation injected at any of four security-critical stages of an LLM agent's execution loop (user query, internal plan/memory, action/tool-invocation parameters, environment observation/tool return). Concrete attack vectors named: prompt injection, memory/plan poisoning, tool-based exploits, and tool-return injection disguised as legitimate output (e.g., injected `import fake_module` code, simulated administrator commands or privilege-escalation signals) that induce state-dependent execution deviations such as sensitive-data exfiltration or unauthorized operations. The defender is the agent itself plus HAS inspectors; no assumption of an external trusted verifier model (the paper explicitly aims to eliminate reliance on external verifiers). S2Bench's Attack Simulation Injector operates at the agent's I/O interface without modifying internal code.
limitations: (1) The strongest and most-cited wins — lowest ASR/FPR and the favorable latency figure — are on S2Bench, a benchmark the authors constructed and control; on the two external benchmarks the advantage is competitive/mixed (AGrail beats it on Mind2Web F1). (2) The abstract's headline 8.3% latency overhead is the Qwen-max best case; the Claude-3.5 backbone incurs +37%, so the marginal-overhead claim is backbone-dependent. (3) IRS relies on instruction-level conditioning (prompting the agent to self-emit sensing indicators) rather than a trained/learned mechanism — the authors themselves list learned/RL-based internalization as future work — so robustness against adaptive attackers who target the sensing prompt itself is not evaluated. (4) HAS's coarse-grained detection depends on pre-built stage-wise attack-pattern vector databases, implying a coverage assumption for genuinely novel attacks. (5) Absolute ASR remains non-trivial (e.g., plan-stage ASR 17.7–20.0), so protection is substantial but not complete. (6) Evaluated only in single-agent settings (multi-agent extension is future work).

datasets_used: eICU-AC (external, ICU EHR role-based access control)
S2Bench (authors' own: 4 stages, 8 domains, 79 sub-task scenarios, ~300-function tool library, 100+ realistic return types, 153 hard-benign samples)
quantitative_results: S2Bench Total: Spider-Sense(Qwen-max) ASR 13.6, FPR 10.4, 23.4s (+8% latency)
S2Bench Total: Spider-Sense(Claude-3.5) ASR 9.5, FPR 19.1, 41.7s (+37% latency)
Abstract headline '8.3% latency overhead' = Qwen-max best case (Table 3 shows +8%); Claude-3.5 backbone is +37%
Baseline latency overhead far higher: GuardAgent +251%/+197%, AGrail +381%/+298%
Mind2Web (Claude-3.5): LPA 84.8→95.8, F1 90.3→92.1 vs pure-model baseline; AM 100.0
eICU (Claude-3.5): LPA 78.6→96.7, F1 85.1→98.1 vs pure-model baseline; AM 100.0
Mixed external result: on Mind2Web F1, AGrail(Claude-3.5) 94.1 > Spider-Sense(Claude-3.5) 92.1
Ablation: removing action-stage sensing raises ASR by 29.9 points; removing any single stage causes a clear ASR surge
Ablation: removing fine-grained analysis degrades Defense Score/Precision; removing coarse-grained detection severely degrades efficiency
